item.content:encoded:
Post country and how much PTO you get >aus >5 weeks holiday 2 weeks sick

Open thread • Replies: 56 • Updated: 2026-07-05 17:29 UTC

OP

Post country and how much PTO you get
>aus
>5 weeks holiday 2 weeks sick

Early replies (first 3)

62443952
US
"Flexible" but I took seven weeks last year

62443958
>>62443952

If you’re white collar professional is the USA really a nightmare for employees like everyone says ? Or it’s over exaggerated?

62443972
>>62443958
Depends on the company I guess. I've always imagined that your average retard that complains about a lack of PTO works fast food, retail, or something equally menial

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62445041
>>62444988

Yea I’m planning to, will just keep 5 days spare just in case

62446526
>>62443950
>Canada
>I work contract so maybe only work 6-8 months of the year then 4 months off with employment insurance but I've had my EI case open for like 5 years now, I just claim it every chance I get.

62446702
15 days per year of combined PTO + sick time as a burger tech worker. It was 13 days per year until I hit one year at the company.
I think I need to get a new job.

62447281
>>62443950

>0 days annual leave
>0 days sick leave
>Unpaid public holidays
>No legal protections if they sack me
The advantage is that I get paid more than my colleagues, and I'm well-known enough in my industry that I'll never be out of work.

62447298
>>62443950
Latvia.
32 days plus unlimited sick leave and also I cam take paid sick leave to take care of my kids if they get sick.

62447300

You might have a huge salary but only 10 to 15 days paid time off and 0 sick leave. That's normal in America. And people are encouraged strongly to take 0 vacation days (can be "cashed out").

62447301
>>62444080
>holidays
Nobody was talking about holidays and now you're coping.
If the rest of us add in Holidays then we get like 50 days PTO a year.

62447310
Notice how America is right in-between or on par with most European countries, yet the doomer grifters on YouTube are always pushing shit about how Americans don't get any days off.

Same with the retarded air-conditioning debate. Now that Europeans are actually visiting Florida and Texas, they're like "oh, sorry Americans, we didn't realize you live in a fucking jungle hellscape".

62447320
>ITT: extreme lying and coping Amerifats

62447342
>>62443950
>CZ
>none, because I'm a business owner
>used to be 4-6 weeks + some sick days + national holidays when I was an employee
>used to be unlimited paid holiday when I worked for a certain gamedev studio as a contractor

62447350
>>62443950
Thank god I'm no longer a wagie but I used to get 27 days PTO which was actually only 20-21 after you account for holidays when the pharmacy was closed anyway. I had to work for 8 years to get to that point. Years 1 to 7 mean you get 5 fewer days. On top of that, there were a bunch of gay rules for when and how time off could get approved. I used to have to swap shifts with a bunch of other pharmacists if I wanted 3 weeks off in a row for international travel.

62447370
>>62447300
>>62447301
I'm in USA

I get 25 - 30 days of paid vacation. If I included Holidays it would be another 10 days. And I'm private sector. Government sector or financial/banking employees get even more holidays than that.

Quit with the seething.

62447386
>>62444126
>USPS
Were federal but independent
>wounded warrior leave 13 days
>annual leave 20 days/ 26 if you've been here like 15 years or something
>sick leave 13 days
>federal holidays are off

62447414
>>62447370
>>62447320
Curious.
I worked for American companies for decades. Northrop Grumman. Booz Allen. GD etc.
Mostly I had 10 to 15 days total PTO and 0 sick leave. With a small company I got up to 22 days total PTO after 5 years tenure.

62447425
>>62447414
Yes, it's based on years of service. Which makes sense. Some loser shouldn't be able to get hired, work for 1 month, take 2 weeks for, get paid, then quit.

62447617
Another amerifat here. I work in biotech. 4 weeks vacation, plus 1 week sick time, but were discouraged from using that unless we’re “””actually sick.””” Fed holidays too, plus they give everyone the week between Christmas and new years off. After one more year there I’ll get 5 weeks vacation.

62447636
>>62447320
People on /biz/ are generally more successful then your average wagie thus better benefits with their job. Your average wagie that talks about stocks is piling into something that already had parabolic growth and is due for a drawdown. Their PoV is worthless

62447666
>>62443950
5 weeks. American but yuro company.

>>62443958
Yes it's one reason I haven't changed jobs. 2 weeks is normal even for good jobs.

62447893
>>62447310
Yeah reading this thread was genuinely surprising. I finally visited Europe (Italy) for the first time over the last 2 weeks and while it was very nice, I don't think life in europe is way better than life in America like I was led to believe. They each have advantages and disadvantages. But with that said, it is a lot easier to accrue personal wealth here than there.
>>62447301
Yeah, I didn't count holidays either even though they're technically PTO.
>>62447298
Doesn't everyone have unlimited sick leave on paper at least? We're only talking about PTO.

62447964
>>62447893
I mean I have 4 kids and when they get sick the pediatrician writes me a note that gives me a week paid off work.

62447974
>>62447964
I can do the same. For up to 4 weeks i don't even need the note.

62447987
>>62447964
>>62447974
Oh, but after 4 weeks i do need the note and it stops being paid.

62447990
>>62447281
How many dicks did you have to suck to get your stellar reputation?

62448091
>>62447974
>>62447987
If I wasn't American I'd probably believe you but alas.

62448115
>>62448091
It's true. My manager is very relaxed about sick leave because he himself uses it every year.

62448199
>>62448115
/thread

Never once in my 15 years of work have I had a manager deny, or get pissed about me using my vacation days. Like I said earlier, this is all doomer nonsense people watch on YouTube or read on left-wing news headlines.

And the Americans that don't get paid leave (like waiters), have open work schedule, unlike factory workers. Waiters can take off or trade shifts with people whenever they want, they just don't get paid for it.

62448201
>>62447617
/thread

62448206
>>62444410
>Also how does it work if you leave the company? Here we get paid out our leave (not sick tho) in cash when we quit.
It's different state by state. In most states, companies don't have to pay out your vacation bank if you leave, but every time I had that I found the company was willing to pay it out.
On the unlimited side that means there's no bank to pay out, but once you get to that level it doesn't make much of a difference unless you leave willingly (at which point you probably got a signing bonus at the new job anyway) or if you get laid off you get a severance package, or if you were fired for misconduct or not doing your job they weren't going to pay it out anyway.

62448231
>>62447281
>nigger slave bragging about job security in the 1800s

62448269
i get 22 days + 12 company holidays + half day fridays in the summer. i don't know how many sick days i get, but i wfh so it's not a big deal.

item.title: House buying — 48 replies — 92.3/hr
item.content:encoded:
House buying

Open thread • Replies: 48 • Updated: 2026-07-05 17:07 UTC

OP

I have $180k sitting in an S&P500 index fund and every normie I meet says I'm making a mistake by not getting a mortgage ASAP because housing will only get more expensive.

What is the /biz/ philosophy on this advice? I'm a rentoid and currently pay only around $1000 a month.

Early replies (first 3)

62447251
>>62447246
>every normie I meet says
You could buy and then the market severely crashes.
Housing shouldn't be a financial asset but that's corporatism for you.

62447257
>>62447251
Yea i guess but the stock market "could" also crash and i lose everything. That's not really insightful advice.

62447265
Lmao you are a rentoid

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62447332
>>62447316
Of course, rentoids all live in properties less than that.

62447333
>>62447325
>stay down for years
>for years
not forever.

I bought for $100k in 02, it was worth about 150 in 08 but dropped to 110 with the market crash. By 12 it was up to 220. Between 12 and 2020 it went up to 750. After 20 it crept down to 600 and that's about where it's at now.

so overall I'm still up 6x overall but I was up 7.5x and it went down some. I don't worry about it because I'm in a resort area and it'll probably be worth over a million in a few years, but it's wrong to think houses can't go down.

62447346
>>62447246
Keep doing what you are doing. Don't listen to the normies. Their house is the only investment they've ever had and it made them money, so they think it's the greatest thing ever. They have no idea that stocks outperform housing every time, because they've never held any stocks.

62447355
>>62447346
part of the problem with your calculations is you're pretending the entire cost of the house can be invested

of course this isn't true because you're still paying rent and that's a 100% loss. So the only portion of your payment that would go to stocks is whatever the difference is between your rent and the mortgage payment. Which usually isn't much.

like if your mortgage is the same as your rent you gain nothing by renting. If your mortgage is $400 more than rent all you gained is whatever the difference is on that $400.

so it depends, but generally long-term buying will always beat renting because rent is a straight loss while the mortgage makes you something.

62447361
>>62447346
also of course by the time you're 20 years into your mortgage, it's going to be MUCH LESS than rent. I currently pay $800/month for a house that would easily rent for $4k per month.

over time the rent goes up while the mortgage remains static. It actually becomes cheaper because inflation eats away at the cost.

62447365
>>62447355
You forgot the down payment. If you put 50k down on a house vs. 50k in the stock market. In 25 years 50k turns into $541,000 in the stock market. That's most of the value of the house right there.

62447374
>>62447365
true, you're right about that. I forget it because I did an FHA with 3% down. At the time I paid $3k down.

google says that would be worth at best around $30k right now vs. a house that's worth over half a million.

also again, as rent goes up and my house payment remains the same I can put increasingly large amounts of cash into other investments.

62447376
>>62447365
also I'm not advising anyone to buy a house. It really depends on the location and how long you stay in it whether it's worth it or not.

over long time spans it has certainly paid off for me, but I don't know what will happen in the future. I do know prices are very high compared to wages right now. But I don't know if that will improve over time or not.

62447379
>>62447376
for example when I bought in 2002 my house cost 3 years wages in my area. Currently it would cost 12 years wages in my area.

based on that alone it's far less affordable to buy a house than it used to be.

62447387
I'm single so I feel like buying a house would be a waste. I also don't plan to live in my area for 15 years.

I do have a VA loan available since I'm a veteran but I don't think that makes a massive difference since I would pay off most of the principle in cash.

These 250k houses in my area look like absolute shit, plus the property tax rate is very high in my area.

I would end up evaporating more cash to live in a completely paid off $250k house (in some shitty suburb) every month then renting my apartment in the middle of the city.

62447395
>>62447387
that's another great point. If you don't need a house then it's probably not worth buying one especially if you plan on moving before the break even point. You'd be spending more money on space you don't need.

overall I tell my kids not to buy if they're not going to stay in place at least 10 years.

I also have VA available but FHA has always been a better deal for me. VA is great for buying a house you plan on renting out, but I've only done that once. If you're not going to rent the place out an FHA may actually be cheaper. In my experience the FHA loans are also much easier than the VA.

62447406
>every normie I meet says I'm making a mistake by not getting a mortgage ASAP because housing will only get more expensive.

They always say this at the top

62447412
>>62447333
So as I said, real estate only goes up in America.
Forever.
Act accordingly.

62447420
>>62447395
Buying to rent seems like not a very good financial decision. I would pocket a couple hundred bucks a month when i could just keep the entire principle in stocks.

62447628
>>62447412
>real estate only goes up in America.
It doesn't though. It can go down and stay down for years
>but not forever
No, not forever. But you aren't going to live in that house forever, so the "muh forever" argument is utterly moot.

62447657
Keep renting until you can purchase outright

Closing costs are 10k+ these days and interest rates are 6%+. Also during negoiations, you may be able to shave an extra 10 to 20k off just because closing on a cash home takes under 2 weeks compared to mortgage which can take 6wk or more. Much easier on sellers so they accept less.

Closing costs on a home with no mortgage is generally the cost of an inspection ($500) plus $250 to the title company. Every other thing the bank normally requires can be skipped

62447660
The opinions of normalfags and people who say "rentoid" are worthless, the US is collapsing. You want 180k to become 1.8 million and then to leave this dump of a country.

62447702
>>62447628
>dips
>oh no no no
Better not invest and rent forever.

62447752
>>62447660
>the US is collapsing

That's an incel fantasy you find pmg losers saying to justify losing all their money to a scam

62447776
>>62447752
Correct. America has another 100 years minimum so long as the CIA and Palantir play things right (they will).

62447823
>>62447246
fast forward 20 years:

birth rates collapsed

boomers dying en mass
the west continues to go downhill

historically house prices have been tied to income. and now apparently AI is going to take all our jobs, so I guess you should take that into account as well. the only thing keeping house prices high is immigration. locals cannot afford 1 million dollars for a house. most western countries would have a negative growth rate without immigration. house prices don't go up endlessly for no reason. even if I could afford a house, I would not buy one. you are tied down, you have to maintain the house, protect the house, insure it, pay the property taxes, utilities.. pay pay pay pay fucking endless bleeding money

62447843

Waaaaaaaaaaaah im a victim Waaaaaaaaaaaah

They just banned Blackrcok from snatching up properties. The plan seems to be to try to keep it stable until the boomers die then let it drop.

item.title: Line will not go down ever again — 7 replies — 28.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
Line will not go down ever again

Open thread • Replies: 7 • Updated: 2026-07-05 17:37 UTC

OP

I think i've just realized something looming over all western markets: we are past the era of market corrections. The line will never go down again.

Check out picrel's graph of the German stock index since 1988. After the dotcom hype of the 90s, the German DAX index topped out at around 7.500. A number it would take seven years to reach again. Thtas how long economic bust and boom cycles would naturally take in the markets.

Next time it would reach 7.500, it would be in 2007, shortly before the global (technically western) financial crisis. It would contract by about 50% but instead of taking seven years, it would only take four years to overtake the former record high.

But now look at what happened ever since: the DAX went from this multi decade high of 7.500 to the current 25.700 within just 13 years. In no way has economic output increased threefold in that time, so something else must be the reason.

After the fallout of the financial crisis, quantitative easing became a standard measure in western economies, to "stabilize" markets. This came at the cost of long term inflationary tendencies. Even after markets were past their old records, the governmental bodies decided to keep going. This had the effect of an ever larger amount of inflationary liquidity driving the stock markets higher indefinitely.

So this is where we are now. The one thing that made me suspicious about the situation, is how the German chancellor recommended to his people to put money into markets. I suspect this comes out of a need to further continue the line going up. Governments face massive inflation eversince the covid pump. It should have corrected markets by 60%, but that was not allowed to happen. As they did in 2008, inflation was chosen as a measure to keep valuations high.

Even more insidious, in a time, where young people are investing like never before, it seems that getting into wealth building in markets is harder than ever before.

Early replies (first 3)

62448196
-cont-

Current situations with the broader power dynamics in the world shifting towards BRICS has me quite sceptical of the current valuations of the western markets. The fact that the people in charge are desperate for te public to put their savings in to markets. The problem that our governments are no longer able to mask the inflationary spending, used to prop up markets. The fact that large amounts of increase in net worth and living standards has gone to a tiny fraction of society, which is now begging for the common man to support their system.

The west has no way out.

Either continue printing money to maintain on paper-growth at the expense of destroying your own currency. Or face the reality that large parts of the shareholder's money is based on fake growth in the stock markets.

What do you think they will do? Begging people to invest their savings seems like a last straw to me. A desperate attempt to somehow keep markets alive.

Only if you buy the ones that are expensive. While Vanguard's VOO is $684/share, Schwab's SWPPX is only $19. Both track the S&P 500 index. Vanguard supports fractional shares for DRIP. Schwab's SWPPX is cheap because Schwab doesn't like its funds to have a high purchase price so they do stock splits whenever the price gets too high (SWPPX recently had a 6:1 split), and it also has fractional shares for DRIP. item.title: UNDER $10K NET WORTH THREAD #1 — 202 replies — 12.9/hr
item.content:encoded:
UNDER $10K NET WORTH THREAD #1

Open thread • Replies: 202 • Updated: 2026-07-05 01:59 UTC

OP

Brothers,

SpaceX has begun to rise. Its success or fall is of little importance to us in the short term.

In the meanwhile we must devise ways to gain a spot at the table with our kin in the six figure club.

I for one propose we spend our time learning a subject. Knowledge is the greatest wealth of all.

However, I would be remiss to not tell the truth that I haven't a clue what we should study.

So instead I suggest we create an eggregore for these threads. Others made monero-chan, bobo and sminem

I am thinking we create one based on a girl who would be willing to believe in us while we are poor and brave the pain throughout having to witness our more disappointing moments of action. Never losing faith in us, she is always there. Yes. This is how we shall spend our time.

At any rate, that is my suggestion for tonight's fun. I have to work in the morning so let's not get too rowdy. Take it or leave it. If you will take it, then be sure to double it and pass it on to the next anon.

Let us commence the general!

Come one, come all!

emperorBTC's telegram has a basic bitch tldr set of material to learn from. also has a youtube where he goes over stuff. that's where I started and I like that he focuses on the absolute basics, although he leans heavily towards confirmation w/ volume which others may disagree with. btc focused but btc/crypto is perhaps the most effective use of TA.
basically there's no need to get fancy and the chart is the final source of truth because what the price does > what people cope and/or seethe about
support/resistance levels and price action with risk management (defined r:r) = compound as you get gud
once you commit you'll realize "the catch" on people actually make money from trading is is simply how retarded the overwhelming majority of people are that they simply cannot manage a few simple tenets to click buttons and make money, you likely being one of them if you don't develop a process and risk too much on trades
gl anon

>>62428130
also perps have exploded so trading equities for crypto natives has been a pleasure to avoid tradfi gatekeeping bs
follow the right people on twatter with alerts set to on and it's like having multiple research firms giving alpha for free-- key word "right" accounts. hopefully you have the pattern recognition to figure out which are spewing paid channel larps and which are providing genuine insight
that's basically the whole game. if you can get into a group chat with other chads that's even better because a network is stronger than you alone, although this is def an arena where it ultimately comes down to you mastering yourself (you can't copy trade your way to wealth but you can learn what others do/how they enter/exit positions)
legend trade is a new perps platform that's gamifying this if you wanna start there instead of hyperliquid (front-end using hyperliquid)
Also bigtrout300 on twitter and join his tg as well he's pretty dope. There's a lot of great accounts posting good shit right now. Some really good posters fade in/out of cycles while the scammers/extractors are always abundant, but just use discretion
